title: Tense Markers
description: Vietnamese verbs never change form — no conjugation, no -ed, no -ing. Instead, time markers like 'đã,' 'đang,' and 'sẽ' sit before the verb to signal past, present, or future. Learn how this elegant system works.

sections.content:
One of the most beautiful things about Vietnamese grammar: **verbs never conjugate.** 'Ăn' means eat, ate, eating, will eat, have eaten — all the same word. Vietnamese handles time through **context** and **optional time markers** placed before or after the verb.

Here's the system:

**Past:**
- **đã** — marks a completed action (like English past tense)
- **rồi** — 'already' (placed at the end of the sentence)
- **mới** — 'just' (something that happened recently)
- **vừa** — 'just now' (even more recent than 'mới')

**Present:**
- **đang** — marks an ongoing action (like English -ing)

**Future:**
- **sẽ** — marks a future action (like English 'will')
- **sắp** — 'about to' (imminent future)

**The secret heritage speakers need to know:** In everyday Southern Vietnamese, people often **drop the tense marker entirely** and rely on context. 'Hôm qua tui ăn phở' (Yesterday I ate pho) — the word 'hôm qua' (yesterday) already tells you it's past tense, so 'đã' is optional. Formal/written Vietnamese uses tense markers more consistently, but in conversation, context does most of the work.

items.notes: Placed before the verb to indicate past tense: 'Tui đã ăn' (I already ate / I have eaten). Often dropped in casual speech when context is clear. More common in written Vietnamese and formal speech. If you include it, it adds emphasis: 'I DID eat.'

Placed before the verb to show an action in progress: 'Tui đang ăn' (I'm eating right now). Unlike 'đã,' this one is regularly used in both speech and writing because it genuinely adds meaning — it tells the listener the action is happening RIGHT NOW.

exampleSentence.english: What are you doing right now?

items.notes: Placed before the verb for future actions: 'Tui sẽ đi' (I will go). Like 'đã,' it can be dropped when context is clear: 'Ngày mai tui đi' (Tomorrow I go) is perfectly natural. 'Sẽ' adds certainty or promise: 'Tui sẽ về' = I WILL come back.

exampleSentence.vietnamese: Tui sẽ gọi cho em sau nha.

items.notes: Placed at the END of a sentence to indicate something is done: 'Ăn rồi' (Already ate). This is probably the tense marker you hear most in Southern Vietnamese families. 'Xong rồi' (Done already), 'Biết rồi' (I already know). Very versatile and very common.

exampleSentence.vietnamese: Con làm bài tập xong rồi.

items.notes: The companion to 'rồi.' 'Rồi' = already done; 'chưa' = not yet done. As a question: 'Ăn chưa?' (Eaten yet?). As an answer: 'Chưa' (Not yet). This pair — rồi/chưa — is one of the most important in everyday Vietnamese.

exampleSentence.english: Have you showered yet? — Not yet.

items.notes: Indicates something happened recently: 'Tui mới tới' (I just arrived). Implies the action was completed not long ago. Different from 'vừa' in that 'mới' can cover a slightly wider time range — anywhere from minutes to days ago.

exampleSentence.vietnamese: Tui mới mua điện thoại mới.
exampleSentence.english: I just bought a new phone.

items.notes: Even more immediate than 'mới.' 'Tui vừa ăn xong' (I just finished eating — like, moments ago). Often combined: 'vừa mới' for emphasis: 'Tui vừa mới tới' (I literally just got here). In Southern speech, 'vừa' and 'mới' are sometimes interchangeable.

exampleSentence.vietnamese: Tui vừa nói chuyện với má.
exampleSentence.english: I just spoke with mom (moments ago).

items.notes: Indicates something is about to happen very soon: 'Sắp mưa rồi' (It's about to rain). Closer in time than 'sẽ.' 'Sẽ' = will (general future), 'sắp' = about to (imminent). 'Sắp tới' = coming soon.

Placed after the verb to indicate completion: 'Ăn xong' (finished eating), 'Làm xong' (finished doing/working). Often paired with 'rồi': 'Ăn xong rồi' (Done eating already). Very common in everyday speech. 'Xong chưa?' = Finished yet?

exampleSentence.vietnamese: Ăn xong rồi mình đi nha.
exampleSentence.english: After we finish eating, let's go.

items.notes: 'Chưa bao giờ' means 'have never (done something)': 'Tui chưa bao giờ đi Việt Nam' (I've never been to Vietnam). It implies there's still a possibility in the future. For absolute 'never,' you'd use 'không bao giờ' (will never).

exampleSentence.vietnamese: Tui chưa bao giờ ăn sầu riêng.

items.notes: Two key meanings: (1) 'always': 'Tui luôn nhớ' (I always remember). (2) 'right away/immediately' in Southern speech: 'Đi luôn!' (Let's go right now!), 'Ăn luôn đi!' (Eat it now!). The second meaning is very Southern.

exampleSentence.vietnamese: Làm luôn đi, đừng để mai.
exampleSentence.english: Do it right now, don't leave it for tomorrow.

sections.content:
**Why Vietnamese doesn't need conjugation:**

English speakers often wonder: without verb conjugation, isn't Vietnamese ambiguous? Not really. Vietnamese uses **context** more than English does. The time word in a sentence usually makes tense obvious:

- **Hôm qua** tui ăn phở. (**Yesterday** I ate pho.) — past is clear
- **Hôm nay** tui ăn phở. (**Today** I eat pho.) — present is clear
- **Ngày mai** tui ăn phở. (**Tomorrow** I'll eat pho.) — future is clear

Time markers (đã, đang, sẽ) are only needed when context alone isn't enough. This is actually more efficient — why conjugate a verb when 'yesterday' already tells you it's past tense?

Heritage speakers have an advantage here: you've been processing Vietnamese without conjugation your whole life. Your brain already handles this naturally. The formal terms (đã, đang, sẽ) are just labels for what you already intuitively understand.

**Tense Marker Quick Reference:**

| Marker | Position | Meaning | Example |
|--------|----------|---------|--------|
| **đã** | before verb | past (completed) | Tui đã ăn. (I ate.) |
| **đang** | before verb | present (ongoing) | Tui đang ăn. (I'm eating.) |
| **sẽ** | before verb | future (will) | Tui sẽ ăn. (I will eat.) |
| **sắp** | before verb | about to | Sắp mưa. (About to rain.) |
| **mới** | before verb | just (recently) | Tui mới ăn. (I just ate.) |
| **vừa** | before verb | just now | Tui vừa ăn. (I just ate — moments ago.) |
| **rồi** | end of sentence | already | Ăn rồi. (Already ate.) |
| **xong** | after verb | finished | Ăn xong. (Finished eating.) |
| **chưa** | before verb / end | not yet | Chưa ăn. / Ăn chưa? |

**Combo moves:**
- 'vừa mới' = just now (emphatic recent past)
- 'xong rồi' = done already
- 'sắp...rồi' = almost (about to be done)
- 'chưa bao giờ' = never (have never)

exercises.question: How do you say 'I'm eating right now' in Vietnamese?
exercises.explanation: 'Đang' marks an action in progress — something happening right now. 'Tui đang ăn' = I'm eating (right now). 'Đã' = past, 'sẽ' = future, 'mới' = just recently.

exercises.question: What's the difference between 'sẽ' and 'sắp'?
options: 'Sẽ' is general future, 'sắp' is imminent future (about to)
exercises.correctAnswer: 'Sẽ' is general future, 'sắp' is imminent future (about to)
exercises.explanation: 'Sẽ' marks general future actions (I will go tomorrow). 'Sắp' means something is about to happen very soon — it's imminent. 'Sắp mưa' = It's about to rain (any second). 'Sẽ mưa' = It will rain (at some point).

exercises.question: How would you answer 'Ăn chưa?' if you already ate?
exercises.explanation: 'Rồi' (already) is the natural answer to a 'chưa' question when the action is done. 'Ăn chưa?' — 'Rồi.' (Already / Yes, already ate.) The 'rồi/chưa' pair works together: 'chưa' asks, 'rồi' confirms completion.

exercises.question: Complete the sentence: 'Tui _____ tới, đợi tui chút.' (I just arrived, wait for me a moment.)
exercises.english: I just arrived, wait for me a moment.
exercises.explanation: 'Mới' or 'vừa' both mean 'just' (recently completed). 'Tui mới tới' = I just arrived. 'Vừa mới' combines both for extra emphasis on how recent the action is. All three answers are natural.

exercises.question: Type 'I've never been to Vietnam' in Vietnamese. (Hint: chưa bao giờ + đi + Việt Nam)
exercises.correctAnswer: Tui chưa bao giờ đi Việt Nam.
acceptableAnswers: Tui chưa bao giờ đi Việt Nam.
Tui chưa bao giờ đi Việt Nam
tui chưa bao giờ đi Việt Nam
Tôi chưa bao giờ đi Việt Nam
exercises.explanation: 'Chưa bao giờ' means 'have never.' 'Tui chưa bao giờ đi Việt Nam' = I have never gone to Vietnam. Using 'chưa' (not yet) instead of 'không' (never/won't) implies there's still a possibility you might go someday.
